code: dict1 <- dictionary(     list(populism=c("elit*", "consensus*", "undemocratic*", "referend*",                     "corrupt*", "propagand", "politici*", "*deceit*",                     "*deceiv*", "*betray*", "shame*", "scandal*", "truth*",                     "dishonest*", "establishm*", "ruling*"))      ) flatten_dictionary(dict1) #> Dictionary object with 1 key entry. #> - [populism]: #>   - elit*, consensus*, undemocratic*, referend*, corrupt*, propagand, politici*, #>     *deceit*, *deceiv*, *betray*, shame*, scandal*, truth*, dishonest*, #>     establishm*, ruling*  dict2 <- dictionary(     list(level1a = list(level1a1 = c("l1a11", "l1a12"),          level1a2 = c("l1a21", "l1a22")),          level1b = list(level1b1 = c("l1b11", "l1b12"),          level1b2 = c("l1b21", "l1b22", "l1b23")),          level1c = list(level1c1a = list(level1c1a1 = c("lowest1", "lowest2")),          level1c1b = list(level1c1b1 = c("lowestalone"))))      ) flatten_dictionary(dict2) #> Dictionary object with 6 key entries. #> - [level1a.level1a1]: #>   - l1a11, l1a12 #> - [level1a.level1a2]: #>   - l1a21, l1a22 #> - [level1b.level1b1]: #>   - l1b11, l1b12 #> - [level1b.level1b2]: #>   - l1b21, l1b22, l1b23 #> - [level1c.level1c1a.level1c1a1]: #>   - lowest1, lowest2 #> - [level1c.level1c1b.level1c1b1]: #>   - lowestalone flatten_dictionary(dict2, 2) #> Dictionary object with 6 key entries. #> - [level1a1]: #>   - l1a11, l1a12 #> - [level1a2]: #>   - l1a21, l1a22 #> - [level1b1]: #>   - l1b11, l1b12 #> - [level1b2]: #>   - l1b21, l1b22, l1b23 #> - [level1c1a]: #>   - lowest1, lowest2 #> - [level1c1b]: #>   - lowestalone flatten_dictionary(dict2, 1:2) #> Dictionary object with 6 key entries. #> - [level1a.level1a1]: #>   - l1a11, l1a12 #> - [level1a.level1a2]: #>   - l1a21, l1a22 #> - [level1b.level1b1]: #>   - l1b11, l1b12 #> - [level1b.level1b2]: #>   - l1b21, l1b22, l1b23 #> - [level1c.level1c1a]: #>   - lowest1, lowest2 #> - [level1c.level1c1b]: #>   - lowestalone

title: Locate keywords-in-context — kwic
text: x character, corpus, tokens object pattern character vector, list character vectors, dictionary, collocations object.  See pattern details. window number context words displayed around keyword valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. separator character separate words output case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values index index object specify keywords ... unused

title: Locate keywords-in-context — kwic
text: pattern keyword pattern phrase, possibly multiple patterns, may include punctuation.  pattern contains whitespace, best wrap phrase() make explicit. However pattern collocations (see quanteda.textstats dictionary object, collocations multi-word dictionary keys automatically considered phrases whitespace-separated element matches token sequence.

title: Locate keywords-in-context — kwic
code: # \donttest{ # single token matching toks <- tokens(data_corpus_inaugural[1:8]) kwic(toks, pattern = "secure*", valuetype = "glob", window = 3) #> Keyword-in-context with 6 matches. #>                                                                        #>       [1797-Adams, 478]  welfare, and | secure  | the blessings of     #>      [1797-Adams, 1512]  nations, and | secured | immortal glory with  #>  [1805-Jefferson, 2367]   , and shall | secure  | to you the           #>     [1817-Monroe, 1754] cherished. To | secure  | us against these     #>     [1817-Monroe, 1814] defense as to | secure  | our cities and       #>     [1817-Monroe, 3009]      I can to | secure  | economy and fidelity kwic(toks, pattern = "secur", valuetype = "regex", window = 3) #> Keyword-in-context with 10 matches. #>                                                          #>  [1789-Washington, 1496] government for the | security | #>        [1797-Adams, 478]       welfare, and |  secure  | #>       [1797-Adams, 1512]       nations, and | secured  | #>   [1805-Jefferson, 2367]        , and shall |  secure  | #>      [1813-Madison, 321]       seas and the | security | #>      [1817-Monroe, 1609]      may form some | security | #>      [1817-Monroe, 1754]      cherished. To |  secure  | #>      [1817-Monroe, 1814]      defense as to |  secure  | #>      [1817-Monroe, 3009]           I can to |  secure  | #>      [1817-Monroe, 3427]           and as a | security | #>                          #>  of their union          #>  the blessings of        #>  immortal glory with     #>  to you the              #>  of an important         #>  against these dangers   #>  us against these        #>  our cities and          #>  economy and fidelity    #>  against foreign dangers kwic(toks, pattern = "security", valuetype = "fixed", window = 3) #> Keyword-in-context with 4 matches. #>                                                          #>  [1789-Washington, 1496] government for the | security | #>      [1813-Madison, 321]       seas and the | security | #>      [1817-Monroe, 1609]      may form some | security | #>      [1817-Monroe, 3427]           and as a | security | #>                          #>  of their union          #>  of an important         #>  against these dangers   #>  against foreign dangers  # phrase matching kwic(toks, pattern = phrase("secur* against"), window = 2) #> Keyword-in-context with 2 matches. #>                                                                          #>  [1817-Monroe, 1609:1610] form some | security against | these dangers   #>  [1817-Monroe, 3427:3428]      as a | security against | foreign dangers kwic(toks, pattern = phrase("war against"), valuetype = "regex", window = 2) #> Keyword-in-context with 1 match. #>                                                              #>  [1801-Jefferson, 1268:1269] the surest | bulwarks against | #>                            #>  antirepublican tendencies  # use index idx <- index(toks, phrase("secur* against")) kwic(toks, index = idx, window = 2) #> Keyword-in-context with 2 matches. #>                                                                          #>  [1817-Monroe, 1609:1610] form some | security against | these dangers   #>  [1817-Monroe, 3427:3428]      as a | security against | foreign dangers # } kw <- kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural[1:20]), "provident*") is.kwic(kw) #> [1] TRUE is.kwic("Not a kwic") #> [1] FALSE is.kwic(kw[, c("pre", "post")]) #> [1] TRUE  toks <- tokens(data_corpus_inaugural[1:8]) kw <- kwic(toks, pattern = "secure*", valuetype = "glob", window = 3) as.data.frame(kw) #>          docname from   to            pre keyword                 post pattern #> 1     1797-Adams  478  478  welfare , and  secure     the blessings of secure* #> 2     1797-Adams 1512 1512  nations , and secured  immortal glory with secure* #> 3 1805-Jefferson 2367 2367    , and shall  secure           to you the secure* #> 4    1817-Monroe 1754 1754 cherished . To  secure     us against these secure* #> 5    1817-Monroe 1814 1814  defense as to  secure       our cities and secure* #> 6    1817-Monroe 3009 3009       I can to  secure economy and fidelity secure*

title: Count the number of tokens or types — ntoken
code: # simple example txt <- c(text1 = "This is a sentence, this.", text2 = "A word. Repeated repeated.") toks <- tokens(txt) ntoken(toks) #> text1 text2  #>     7     6  ntype(toks) #> text1 text2  #>     7     5  ntoken(tokens_tolower(toks))  # same #> text1 text2  #>     7     6  ntype(tokens_tolower(toks))   # fewer types #> text1 text2  #>     6     4   # with some real texts toks <- tokens(corpus_subset(data_corpus_inaugural, Year < 1806)) ntoken(tokens(toks, remove_punct = TRUE)) #> 1789-Washington 1793-Washington      1797-Adams  1801-Jefferson  1805-Jefferson  #>            1430             135            2318            1726            2166  ntype(tokens(toks, remove_punct = TRUE)) #> 1789-Washington 1793-Washington      1797-Adams  1801-Jefferson  1805-Jefferson  #>             617              91             819             711             799  ntoken(dfm(toks)) #> 1789-Washington 1793-Washington      1797-Adams  1801-Jefferson  1805-Jefferson  #>            1537             147            2577            1923            2380  ntype(dfm(toks)) #> 1789-Washington 1793-Washington      1797-Adams  1801-Jefferson  1805-Jefferson  #>             603              95             801             687             781

what: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
title: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
text: Developer function match patterns quanteda objects token types.

title: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
code: object2id(   x,   types,   valuetype = c("glob", "fixed", "regex"),   case_insensitive = TRUE,   concatenator = "_",   levels = 1,   match_pattern = c("any", "single", "multi"),   keep_nomatch = FALSE )  object2fixed(   x,   types,   valuetype = c("glob", "fixed", "regex"),   case_insensitive = TRUE,   concatenator = "_",   levels = 1,   match_pattern = c("any", "single", "multi"),   keep_nomatch = FALSE )

title: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
text: x list character vectors, dictionary collocations object types token types patterns matched valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values concatenator concatenation character joins multi-word expression types levels integers specifying levels entries hierarchical dictionary applied.  top level 1, subsequent levels describe lower nesting levels.  Values may combined, even levels contiguous, e.g. levels = c(1:3) collapse second level first, record third level (present) collapsed first (see examples). match_pattern select single-word patterns multi-word patterns matched. "", matches single-word multi-word patterns. keep_nomatch keep patterns match

title: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
text: object2fixed() returns list character vectors matched types. object2id() returns list indices matched types attributes. "pattern" attribute records indices matched patterns x; "key" attribute records keys matched patterns x dictionary.

title: Match quanteda objects against token types — object2id
code: types <- c("A", "AA", "B", "BB", "B_B", "C", "C-C")  # dictionary dict <- dictionary(list(A = c("a", "aa"),                          B = c("BB", "B B"),                         C = c("C", "C-C"))) object2fixed(dict, types) #> $A #> [1] "A" #>  #> $A #> [1] "AA" #>  #> $B #> [1] "BB" #>  #> $B #> [1] "B" "B" #>  #> $B #> [1] "B_B" #>  #> $C #> [1] "C" #>  #> $C #> [1] "C-C" #>  object2fixed(dict, types, match_pattern = "single") #> $A #> [1] "A" #>  #> $A #> [1] "AA" #>  #> $B #> [1] "BB" #>  #> $B #> [1] "B_B" #>  #> $C #> [1] "C" #>  #> $C #> [1] "C-C" #>  object2fixed(dict, types, match_pattern = "multi") #> $B #> [1] "B" "B" #>   # phrase pats <- phrase(c("a", "aa", "zz", "bb", "b b")) object2fixed(pats, types) #> $a #> [1] "A" #>  #> $aa #> [1] "AA" #>  #> $bb #> [1] "BB" #>  #> $`b b` #> [1] "B" "B" #>  object2fixed(pats, types, keep_nomatch = TRUE) #> $a #> [1] "A" #>  #> $aa #> [1] "AA" #>  #> $zz #> character(0) #>  #> $bb #> [1] "BB" #>  #> $`b b` #> [1] "B" "B" #>

what: Pattern for feature, token and keyword matching — pattern
title: Pattern for feature, token and keyword matching — pattern
text: Pattern(s) use matching features, tokens, keywords valuetype pattern.

title: Pattern for feature, token and keyword matching — pattern
text: pattern character vector, list character vectors, dictionary, collocations object.  See pattern details.

title: Pattern for feature, token and keyword matching — pattern
text: pattern argument vector patterns, including sequences, match target object, whose match type specified valuetype. Note empty pattern ("") match "padding" tokens object. character character vector token patterns selected removed. Whitespace privileged, character vector, white space interpreted literally. wish consider whitespace-separated elements sequences tokens, wrap argument phrase(). list character objects list elements character vectors length 1, equivalent vector characters.  list element contains vector characters longer length 1, matching consider sequences matches, equivalent wrapping argument phrase(), except matching dfm features apply. dictionary Values dictionary used patterns, literal matches. Multi-word values automatically converted phrases, performing selection compounding using dictionary wrapping dictionary phrase(). collocations Collocations objects created quanteda.textstats::textstat_collocations(), treated phrases automatically.

title: Pattern for feature, token and keyword matching — pattern
code: # these are interpreted literally (patt1 <- c("president", "white house", "house of representatives")) #> [1] "president"                "white house"              #> [3] "house of representatives" # as multi-word sequences phrase(patt1) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white" "house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house"           "of"              "representatives" #>   # three single-word patterns (patt2 <- c("president", "white_house", "house_of_representatives")) #> [1] "president"                "white_house"              #> [3] "house_of_representatives" phrase(patt2) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white_house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house_of_representatives" #>   # this is equivalent to phrase(patt1) (patt3 <- list(c("president"), c("white", "house"),                c("house", "of", "representatives"))) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white" "house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house"           "of"              "representatives" #>   # glob expression can be used phrase(patt4 <- c("president?", "white house", "house * representatives")) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president?" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white" "house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house"           "*"               "representatives" #>   # this is equivalent to phrase(patt4) (patt5 <- list(c("president?"), c("white", "house"), c("house", "*", "representatives"))) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president?" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white" "house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house"           "*"               "representatives" #>   # dictionary with multi-word matches (dict1 <- dictionary(list(us = c("president", "white house", "house of representatives")))) #> Dictionary object with 1 key entry. #> - [us]: #>   - president, white house, house of representatives phrase(dict1) #> [[1]] #> [1] "president" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "white" "house" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "house"           "of"              "representatives" #>

title: Declare a pattern to be a sequence of separate patterns — phrase
text: x character, dictionary, list, collocations, tokens object; compound patterns treated sequence separated separator. list, collocations, tokens objects, use .phrase(). separator character; character patterns. defaults " ".  phrase() .

title: Declare a pattern to be a sequence of separate patterns — phrase
text: phrase() .phrase() return specially classed list whose elements split separate character (pattern) elements. .phrase returns TRUE object created phrase(); FALSE otherwise.

title: Declare a pattern to be a sequence of separate patterns — phrase
code: # make phrases from characters phrase(c("natural language processing")) #> [[1]] #> [1] "natural"    "language"   "processing" #>  phrase(c("natural_language_processing", "text_analysis"), separator = "_") #> [[1]] #> [1] "natural"    "language"   "processing" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "text"     "analysis" #>   # from a dictionary phrase(dictionary(list(catone = c("a b"), cattwo = "c d e", catthree = "f"))) #> [[1]] #> [1] "a" "b" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "c" "d" "e" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "f" #>   # from a list as.phrase(list(c("natural", "language", "processing"))) #> [[1]] #> [1] "natural"    "language"   "processing" #>   # from tokens as.phrase(tokens("natural language processing")) #> [[1]] #> [1] "natural"    "language"   "processing" #>

The Government believes th..." #>  #> Conservative : #> "Attract the brightest and best to our co..." #>  #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 6 more documents ]  toks <- tokens(corp) print(toks, max_ndoc = 3, max_ntoken = 6) #> Tokens consisting of 9 documents. #> BNP : #> [1] "IMMIGRATION"  ":"            "AN"           "UNPARALLELED" "CRISIS"       #> [6] "WHICH"        #> [ ... and 3,274 more ] #>  #> Coalition : #> [1] "IMMIGRATION" "." what: An R package for the quantitative analysis of textual data — quanteda-package
title: An R package for the quantitative analysis of textual data — quanteda-package
text: Functions creating managing textual corpora, extracting features textual data, analyzing features using quantitative methods. fast, flexible, comprehensive framework quantitative text analysis R. Provides functionality corpus management, creating manipulating tokens n-grams, exploring keywords context, forming manipulating sparse matrices documents features feature co-occurrences, analyzing keywords, computing feature similarities distances, applying content dictionaries, applying supervised unsupervised machine learning, visually representing text text analyses, .

title: An R package for the quantitative analysis of textual data — quanteda-package
text: quanteda makes easy manage texts form corpus, defined collection texts includes document-level variables specific text, well meta-data. quanteda includes tools make easy fast manipulate texts corpus, performing common natural language processing tasks simply quickly, tokenizing, stemming, forming ngrams. quanteda's functions tokenizing texts forming multiple tokenized documents document-feature matrix extremely fast simple use. quanteda can segment texts easily words, paragraphs, sentences, even user-supplied delimiters tags. Built text processing functions stringi package, turn built C++ implementation ICU libraries Unicode text handling, quanteda pays special attention fast correct implementation Unicode handling text character set. quanteda built efficiency speed, design around three infrastructures: stringi package text processing, Matrix package sparse matrix objects, computationally intensive processing (e.g. tokens) handled parallelized C++. can fit memory, quanteda handle quickly. (eventually, make possible process objects even larger available memory.) quanteda principally designed allow users fast convenient method go corpus texts selected matrix documents features, defining documents features. package makes easy redefine documents, instance splitting sentences paragraphs, tags, well group larger documents document variables, subset based logical conditions combinations document variables. package also implements common NLP feature selection functions, removing stopwords stemming numerous languages, selecting words found dictionaries, treating words equivalent based user-defined "thesaurus", trimming weighting features based document frequency, feature frequency, related measures tf-idf. Tools working dictionaries one quanteda's principal strengths, package includes several core functions preparing applying dictionaries texts, example lexicon-based sentiment analysis. constructed, quanteda document-feature matrix ("dfm") can easily analyzed using either quanteda's built-tools scaling document positions, used number text analytic tools, : topic models (including converters direct use topicmodels, LDA, stm packages) document scaling (using quanteda.textmodels package's functions "wordfish" "Wordscores" models, direct use ca package correspondence analysis), machine learning variety packages take matrix matrix-like inputs. quanteda includes functions converting core objects, especially dfm, formats easy use analytic packages. Additional features quanteda include: powerful, flexible tools working dictionaries; ability identify keywords associated documents groups documents; ability explore texts using keywords--context; quick computation word document statistics, using quanteda.textstats package, clustering compute distances purposes; comprehensive suite descriptive statistics text number sentences, words, characters, syllables per document; flexible, easy use graphical tools portray many analyses available package.

title: Get or set package options for quanteda — quanteda_options
text: ... options set, key-value pair, options(). may list valid key-value pairs, useful setting group options (see examples). reset logical; TRUE, reset quanteda options default values initialize logical; TRUE, reset quanteda options already defined.  Used setting initial values defined previously, .Rprofile.

title: Get or set package options for quanteda — quanteda_options
text: called using key = value pair (key can label quoted character name)), option set TRUE returned invisibly. called arguments, named list package options returned. called reset = TRUE argument, arguments options reset default values, TRUE returned invisibly.

title: Get or set package options for quanteda — quanteda_options
text: Currently available options : verbose logical; TRUE use default functions verbose argument threads integer; specifies number threads use parallelized functions; defaults maximum number threads print_dfm_max_ndoc, print_corpus_max_ndoc, print_tokens_max_ndoc integer; specify number documents display using defaults printing dfm, corpus, tokens object print_dfm_max_nfeat, print_corpus_max_nchar, print_tokens_max_ntoken integer; specifies number features display printing dfm, number characters display printing corpus documents, number tokens display printing tokens objects print_dfm_summary integer; specifies number documents display using defaults printing dfm print_dictionary_max_nkey, print_dictionary_max_nval number keys values (respectively) display printing dictionary print_kwic_max_nrow number rows display printing kwic object base_docname character; stem name documents unnamed corpus, tokens, dfm created dfm converted another object base_featname character; stem name features unnamed added, whatever reason, dfm operation adds features base_compname character; stem name components created matrix factorization language_stemmer character; language option char_wordstem(), tokens_wordstem(), dfm_wordstem() pattern_hashtag, pattern_username character; regex patterns (social media) hashtags usernames respectively, used avoid segmenting default internal "word" tokenizer tokens_block_size integer; specifies number documents tokenized time blocked tokenization. number large, tokenization becomes faster also memory-intensive. tokens_locale character; specify locale stringi boundary detection tokenization corpus reshaping. See stringi::stri_opts_brkiter(). tokens_tokenizer_word character; current word tokenizer version used default = "word" tokens(), one "word1", "word2", "word3" ("word2"), "word4".

title: Get or set package options for quanteda — quanteda_options

attr(*, "meta")=List of 3 #>   ..$ system:List of 5 #>   .. ..$ package-version:Classes 'package_version', 'numeric_version'  hidden list of 1 #>   .. .. ..$ : int [1:3] 4 4 1 #>   .. ..$ r-version      :Classes 'R_system_version', 'package_version', 'numeric_version'  hidden list of 1 #>   .. .. ..$ : int [1:3] 4 6 1 #>   .. ..$ system         : Named chr [1:3] "Windows" "x86-64" "watan" #>   .. .. ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:3] "sysname" "machine" "user" #>   .. ..$ directory      : chr "C:/Users/watan/Repo/quanteda/docs/reference" #>   .. ..$ created        : Date[1:1], format: "2026-07-18" #>   ..$ object:List of 7 #>   .. ..$ unit        : chr "documents" #>   .. ..$ what        : chr "word" #>   .. ..$ tokenizer   : chr "tokenize_word4" #>   .. ..$ ngram       : int 1 #>   .. ..$ skip        : int 0 #>   .. ..$ concatenator: chr "_" #>   .. ..$ summary     :List of 2 #>   .. .. ..$ hash: chr(0)  #>   .. .. ..$ data: NULL #>   ..$ user  : list() toks[c(1,3)] #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "one"   "two"   "three" #>  #> d3 : #> [1] "seven" "eight" #>  # combining tokens toks1 <- tokens(c(doc1 = "a b c d e", doc2 = "f g h")) toks2 <- tokens(c(doc3 = "1 2 3")) toks1 + toks2 #> Tokens consisting of 3 documents. #> doc1 : #> [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "e" #>  #> doc2 : #> [1] "f" "g" "h" #>  #> doc3 : #> [1] "1" "2" "3" #>  c(toks1, toks2) #> Tokens consisting of 3 documents. #> doc1 : #> [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "e" #>  #> doc2 : #> [1] "f" "g" "h" #>  #> doc3 : #> [1] "1" "2" "3" #>

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
text: Construct tokens object, either importing named list characters external tokenizer, calling internal quanteda tokenizer. tokens() can also applied tokens class objects, means removal rules can applied post-tokenization, although noted possible remove things present.  instance, tokens object already punctuation removed, tokens(x, remove_punct = TRUE) additional effect.

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
code: tokens(   x,   what = "word",   remove_punct = FALSE,   remove_symbols = FALSE,   remove_numbers = FALSE,   remove_url = FALSE,   remove_separators = TRUE,   split_hyphens = FALSE,   split_tags = FALSE,   normalize = FALSE,   include_docvars = TRUE,   padding = FALSE,   concatenator = "_",   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose"),   ...,   xptr = FALSE )

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
text: x input object tokens constructor; tokens, corpus character object tokenize. character; tokenizer use.  default = "word" current version quanteda tokenizer, set quanteda_options(tokens_tokenizer_word). Legacy tokenizers (version < 2) also supported, including default = "word4". See Details quanteda Tokenizers . remove_punct logical; TRUE remove characters Unicode "Punctuation" [P] class, exceptions used prefixes valid social media tags preserve_tags = TRUE remove_symbols logical; TRUE remove characters Unicode "Symbol" [S] class remove_numbers logical; TRUE remove tokens consist numbers, words start digits, e.g. 2day remove_url logical; TRUE removes URLs (http, https, ftp, sftp) email addresses. remove_separators logical; TRUE remove separators separator characters (Unicode "Separator" [Z] "Control" [C] categories) split_hyphens logical; FALSE, split words connected hyphenation hyphenation-like characters words, e.g. "self-aware" becomes c("self", "-", "aware") split_tags logical; FALSE, split social media tags defined quanteda_options(). default patterns pattern_hashtag = "#\\w+#?" pattern_username = "@[-zA-Z0-9_]+". normalize logical; TRUE, Unicode quotation marks hyphens replaced ASCII equivalent. See details. include_docvars TRUE, pass docvars tokens object. apply input character data list characters. padding TRUE, leave empty string removed tokens previously existed.  useful positional match needed pre- post-selected tokens, instance window adjacency needs computed. concatenator character; concatenation character connect tokens making multi-token sequence. verbose TRUE, print timing messages console. ... used pass arguments among functions. xptr TRUE, returns tokens_xptr class object.

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
text: quanteda tokens class object, default serialized list integers corresponding vector types.

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
text: version 2, choice tokenizer left user, tokens() treated constructor (named list) tokenizer. allows users use tokenizer returns named list, use input tokens(), removal splitting rules applied constructed (passed arguments).  removal splitting rules conservative remove split anything, however, unless user requests . usually want split hyphenated words social media tags, extra steps required preserve special tokens. many random characters texts, split_hyphens = TRUE split_tags = TRUE avoid slowdown tokenization. Using external tokenizers best done piping output tokenizers tokens() constructor, additional removal splitting options applied construction stage.  effect, however, tokens exist removal specified tokens() call.  instance, impossible remove punctuation input list tokens() already punctuation tokens removed external tokenization stage. construct tokens object list additional processing, call .tokens() instead tokens(). Recommended tokenizers tokenizers package, generally faster default (built-) tokenizer always splits infix hyphens, spacyr.  default tokenizer quanteda smart, however, special requirements, works extremely well languages well text social media (including hashtags usernames). normalize = TRUE, Unicode characters replaced ASCII equivalent make pattern matching (e.g. stop words) easier: [\u201C\u201D\u201F] double quotation; [\u2018\u201B\u2019] single quotation mark; \u002D\u2010\u2011\u2012\u2013\u2014\u2015] hyphen.

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
text: default word tokenizer = "word" updated major version 4.  even smarter v2 v3 versions, additional options customization.  See tokenize_word4() full details. default tokenizer splits tokens using stri_split_boundaries(x, type = "word") default preserves infix hyphens (e.g. "self-funding"), URLs, social media "tag" characters (#hashtags @usernames), email addresses.  rules defining valid "tag" can found https://www.hashtags.org/featured/-characters-can--hashtag-include/ hashtags https://help.twitter.com/en/managing--account/twitter-username-rules usernames. backward compatibility, following older tokenizers also supported : "word1" (legacy) implements similar behaviour version = "word" found pre-version 2. (preserves social media tags infix hyphens, splits URLs.) "word1" also slower "word2" "word4".  "word1", argument remove_twitter controlled whether social media tags preserved removed, even remove_punct = TRUE. argument longer functional versions >= 2, equivalent control can using split_tags argument selective tokens removals. "word2", "word3" (legacy) implements similar behaviour versions "word" found quanteda versions 3 4. "fasterword" (legacy) splits whitespace control characters, using stringi::stri_split_charclass(x, "[\\p{Z}\\p{C}]+") "fastestword" (legacy) splits space character, using stringi::stri_split_fixed(x, " ") "character" tokenization individual characters "sentence" sentence segmenter based stri_split_boundaries, additional rules avoid splits words like "Mr." otherwise incorrectly detected sentence boundaries.  better sentence tokenization, consider using spacyr.

title: Construct a tokens object — tokens
code: txt <- c(doc1 = "A sentence, showing how tokens() works.",          doc2 = "@quantedainit and #textanalysis https://example.com?p=123.",          doc3 = "Self-documenting code??",          doc4 = "£1,000,000 for 50¢ is gr8 4ever \U0001f600") tokens(txt) #> Tokens consisting of 4 documents. #> doc1 : #>  [1] "A"        "sentence" ","        "showing"  "how"      "tokens"   #>  [7] "("        ")"        "works"    "."        #>  #> doc2 : #> [1] "@quantedainit"              "and"                        #> [3] "#textanalysis"              "https://example.com?p=123." #>  #> doc3 : #> [1] "Self-documenting" "code"             "?"                "?"                #>  #> doc4 : #> [1] "£"         "1,000,000" "for"       "50"        "¢"         "is"        #> [7] "gr8"       "4ever"     "😀"        #>  tokens(txt, what = "word1") #> Tokens consisting of 4 documents. #> doc1 : #>  [1] "A"        "sentence" ","        "showing"  "how"      "tokens"   #>  [7] "("        ")"        "works"    "."        #>  #> doc2 : #>  [1] "@quantedainit" "and"           "#textanalysis" "https"         #>  [5] ":"             "/"             "/"             "example.com"   #>  [9] "?"             "p"             "="             "123"           #> [ ... and 1 more ] #>  #> doc3 : #> [1] "Self-documenting" "code"             "?"                "?"                #>  #> doc4 : #> [1] "£"         "1,000,000" "for"       "50"        "¢"         "is"        #> [7] "gr8"       "4ever"     "😀"        #>   # removing punctuation marks but keeping tags and URLs tokens(txt[1:2], remove_punct = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> doc1 : #> [1] "A"        "sentence" "showing"  "how"      "tokens"   "works"    #>  #> doc2 : #> [1] "@quantedainit"              "and"                        #> [3] "#textanalysis"              "https://example.com?p=123." #>   # splitting hyphenated words tokens(txt[3]) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> doc3 : #> [1] "Self-documenting" "code"             "?"                "?"                #>  tokens(txt[3], split_hyphens = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> doc3 : #> [1] "Self"        "-"           "documenting" "code"        "?"           #> [6] "?"           #>   # symbols and numbers tokens(txt[4]) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> doc4 : #> [1] "£"         "1,000,000" "for"       "50"        "¢"         "is"        #> [7] "gr8"       "4ever"     "😀"        #>  tokens(txt[4], remove_numbers = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> doc4 : #> [1] "£"     "for"   "¢"     "is"    "gr8"   "4ever" "😀"    #>  tokens(txt[4], remove_numbers = TRUE, remove_symbols = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> doc4 : #> [1] "for"   "is"    "gr8"   "4ever" #>   if (FALSE) # using other tokenizers tokens(tokenizers::tokenize_words(txt[4]), remove_symbols = TRUE) tokenizers::tokenize_words(txt, lowercase = FALSE, strip_punct = FALSE) |>     tokens(remove_symbols = TRUE) #> Error in loadNamespace(x): there is no package called 'tokenizers' tokenizers::tokenize_characters(txt[3], strip_non_alphanum = FALSE) |>     tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) #> Error in loadNamespace(x): there is no package called 'tokenizers' tokenizers::tokenize_sentences(     "The quick brown fox.  It jumped over the lazy dog.") |>     tokens() #> Error in loadNamespace(x): there is no package called 'tokenizers'  # \dontrun{}

title: Annotate a tokens object using a dictionary — tokens_annotate
text: x tokens object dictionary applied dictionary dictionary-class object applied x levels integers specifying levels entries hierarchical dictionary applied.  top level 1, subsequent levels describe lower nesting levels.  Values may combined, even levels contiguous, e.g. levels = c(1:3) collapse second level first, record third level (present) collapsed first (see examples). valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values marker characters added dictionary keys create tags. capkeys TRUE, convert dictionary keys uppercase distinguish unmatched tokens. nested_scope treat matches different dictionary keys nested.  one value nested within another, "b" nested within "b c", tokens_lookup() match longer.  nested_scope = "key", longer-match priority applied within key, "dictionary" applies across keys, matching key longer pattern, matches nested within longer pattern keys.  See Details. apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Annotate a tokens object using a dictionary — tokens_annotate
code: txt <- c(d1 = "The United States has the Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean.",          d2 = "Britain and Ireland have the Irish Sea and the English Channel.") toks <- tokens(txt) dict <- dictionary(list(US = list(Countries = c("States"),                                   oceans = c("Atlantic", "Pacific")),                         Europe = list(Countries = c("Britain", "Ireland"),                                       oceans = list(west = "Irish Sea",                                                     east = "English Channel")))) tokens_annotate(toks, dict) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #>  [1] "The"            "United"         "States"         "#US.COUNTRIES#" #>  [5] "has"            "the"            "Atlantic"       "#US.OCEANS#"    #>  [9] "Ocean"          "and"            "the"            "Pacific"        #> [ ... and 3 more ] #>  #> d2 : #>  [1] "Britain"              "#EUROPE.COUNTRIES#"   "and"                  #>  [4] "Ireland"              "#EUROPE.COUNTRIES#"   "have"                 #>  [7] "the"                  "Irish"                "Sea"                  #> [10] "#EUROPE.OCEANS.WEST#" "and"                  "the"                  #> [ ... and 4 more ] #>

code: tokens_chunk(   x,   size,   overlap = 0,   use_docvars = TRUE,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Segment tokens object by chunks of a given size — tokens_chunk
text: x tokens object whose token elements segmented chunks size integer; token length chunks overlap integer; number tokens chunk taken last overlap tokens preceding chunk use_docvars TRUE, repeat docvar values chunk; FALSE, drop docvars chunked tokens verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Segment tokens object by chunks of a given size — tokens_chunk
text: tokens object whose documents split chunks length size.

title: Segment tokens object by chunks of a given size — tokens_chunk
code: txts <- c(doc1 = "Fellow citizens, I am again called upon by the voice of                   my country to execute the functions of its Chief Magistrate.",           doc2 = "When the occasion proper for it shall arrive, I shall                   endeavor to express the high sense I entertain of this                   distinguished honor.") toks <- tokens(txts) tokens_chunk(toks, size = 5) #> Tokens consisting of 10 documents. #> doc1.1 : #> [1] "Fellow"   "citizens" ","        "I"        "am"       #>  #> doc1.2 : #> [1] "again"  "called" "upon"   "by"     "the"    #>  #> doc1.3 : #> [1] "voice"   "of"      "my"      "country" "to"      #>  #> doc1.4 : #> [1] "execute"   "the"       "functions" "of"        "its"       #>  #> doc1.5 : #> [1] "Chief"      "Magistrate" "."          #>  #> doc2.1 : #> [1] "When"     "the"      "occasion" "proper"   "for"      #>  #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 4 more documents ] tokens_chunk(toks, size = 5, overlap = 4) #> Tokens consisting of 47 documents. #> doc1.1 : #> [1] "Fellow"   "citizens" ","        "I"        "am"       #>  #> doc1.2 : #> [1] "citizens" ","        "I"        "am"       "again"    #>  #> doc1.3 : #> [1] ","      "I"      "am"     "again"  "called" #>  #> doc1.4 : #> [1] "I"      "am"     "again"  "called" "upon"   #>  #> doc1.5 : #> [1] "am"     "again"  "called" "upon"   "by"     #>  #> doc1.6 : #> [1] "again"  "called" "upon"   "by"     "the"    #>  #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 41 more documents ]

what: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
text: Replace multi-token sequences multi-word, "compound" token.  resulting compound tokens represent phrase multi-word expression, concatenated concatenator (default, "_" character) form single "token".  ensures sequences processed subsequently single tokens, instance constructing dfm.

title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
code: tokens_compound(   x,   pattern,   valuetype = c("glob", "regex", "fixed"),   concatenator = concat(x),   window = 0L,   case_insensitive = TRUE,   join = TRUE,   keep_unigrams = FALSE,   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
text: x input tokens object pattern character vector, list character vectors, dictionary, collocations object.  See pattern details. valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. concatenator character; concatenation character connect tokens making multi-token sequence. window integer; vector length 1 2 specifies size window tokens adjacent pattern compounded matches pattern.  window can asymmetric two elements specified, first giving window size pattern second window size .  paddings (empty "" tokens) found, window shrunk exclude . case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values join logical; TRUE, join overlapping compounds single compound; otherwise, form separately.  See examples. keep_unigrams TRUE, keep original tokens. apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
text: tokens object token sequences matching pattern replaced new compounded "tokens" joined concatenator.

title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
text: Patterns compounded (naturally) consist multi-word sequences, expected pattern specific.  elements compounded supplied space-delimited elements character vector, wrap vector phrase().  elements compounded separate elements character vector, supply list list element sequence character elements. See examples .

title: Convert token sequences into compound tokens — tokens_compound
code: txt <- "The United Kingdom is leaving the European Union." toks <- tokens(txt, remove_punct = TRUE)  # character vector - not compounded tokens_compound(toks, c("United", "Kingdom", "European", "Union")) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"      "United"   "Kingdom"  "is"       "leaving"  "the"      "European" #> [8] "Union"    #>   # elements separated by spaces - not compounded tokens_compound(toks, c("United Kingdom", "European Union")) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"      "United"   "Kingdom"  "is"       "leaving"  "the"      "European" #> [8] "Union"    #>   # list of characters - is compounded tokens_compound(toks, list(c("United", "Kingdom"), c("European", "Union"))) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"            "United_Kingdom" "is"             "leaving"        #> [5] "the"            "European_Union" #>   # elements separated by spaces, wrapped in phrase() - is compounded tokens_compound(toks, phrase(c("United Kingdom", "European Union"))) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"            "United_Kingdom" "is"             "leaving"        #> [5] "the"            "European_Union" #>   # supplied as values in a dictionary (same as list) - is compounded # (keys do not matter) tokens_compound(toks, dictionary(list(key1 = "United Kingdom",                                       key2 = "European Union"))) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"            "United_Kingdom" "is"             "leaving"        #> [5] "the"            "European_Union" #>  # pattern as dictionaries with glob matches tokens_compound(toks, dictionary(list(key1 = c("U* K*"))), valuetype = "glob") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"            "United_Kingdom" "is"             "leaving"        #> [5] "the"            "European"       "Union"          #>   # note the differences caused by join = FALSE compounds <- list(c("the", "European"), c("European", "Union")) tokens_compound(toks, pattern = compounds, join = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"                "United"             "Kingdom"            #> [4] "is"                 "leaving"            "the_European_Union" #>  tokens_compound(toks, pattern = compounds, join = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"            "United"         "Kingdom"        "is"             #> [5] "leaving"        "the_European"   "European_Union" #>   # use window to form ngrams tokens_remove(toks, pattern = stopwords("en")) |>     tokens_compound(pattern = "leav*", join = FALSE, window = c(0, 3)) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "United"                 "Kingdom"                "leaving_European_Union" #>

code: tokens_group(   x,   groups = docid(x),   fill = FALSE,   env = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Combine documents in a tokens object by a grouping variable — tokens_group
text: x tokens object groups grouping variable sampling, equal length number documents. evaluated docvars data.frame, docvars may referred name without quoting. also changes previous behaviours groups. See news(Version >= "3.0", package = "quanteda") details. fill logical; TRUE groups factor, use levels factor forming new documents grouped object.  result new "document" empty content levels observed, empty document may needed.  groups factor dates, instance, fill = TRUE ensures new object consist one new "document" date, regardless whether documents previously existed date.  effect groups variable(s) factors. env environment list object x searched. Passed substitute non-standard evaluation. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Combine documents in a tokens object by a grouping variable — tokens_group
text: tokens object whose documents equal unique group combinations, whose tokens concatenations tokens group. Document-level variables variation within groups saved docvars.  Document-level variables lists dropped grouping, even exhibit variation within groups.

title: Combine documents in a tokens object by a grouping variable — tokens_group
code: corp <- corpus(c("a a b", "a b c c", "a c d d", "a c c d"),                docvars = data.frame(grp = c("grp1", "grp1", "grp2", "grp2"))) toks <- tokens(corp) tokens_group(toks, groups = grp) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> grp1 : #> [1] "a" "a" "b" "a" "b" "c" "c" #>  #> grp2 : #> [1] "a" "c" "d" "d" "a" "c" "c" "d" #>  tokens_group(toks, groups = c(1, 1, 2, 2)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> 1 : #> [1] "a" "a" "b" "a" "b" "c" "c" #>  #> 2 : #> [1] "a" "c" "d" "d" "a" "c" "c" "d" #>   # with fill tokens_group(toks, groups = factor(c(1, 1, 2, 2), levels = 1:3)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> 1 : #> [1] "a" "a" "b" "a" "b" "c" "c" #>  #> 2 : #> [1] "a" "c" "d" "d" "a" "c" "c" "d" #>  tokens_group(toks, groups = factor(c(1, 1, 2, 2), levels = 1:3), fill = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 3 documents and 1 docvar. #> 1 : #> [1] "a" "a" "b" "a" "b" "c" "c" #>  #> 2 : #> [1] "a" "c" "d" "d" "a" "c" "c" "d" #>  #> 3 : #> character(0) #>

text: Convert tokens equivalence classes defined values dictionary object.

title: Apply a dictionary to a tokens object — tokens_lookup
code: tokens_lookup(   x,   dictionary,   levels = 1:5,   valuetype = c("glob", "regex", "fixed"),   case_insensitive = TRUE,   capkeys = !exclusive,   exclusive = TRUE,   nomatch = NULL,   append_key = FALSE,   separator = "/",   concatenator = concat(x),   nested_scope = c("key", "dictionary"),   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Apply a dictionary to a tokens object — tokens_lookup
text: x tokens object dictionary applied dictionary dictionary-class object applied x levels integers specifying levels entries hierarchical dictionary applied.  top level 1, subsequent levels describe lower nesting levels.  Values may combined, even levels contiguous, e.g. levels = c(1:3) collapse second level first, record third level (present) collapsed first (see examples). valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values capkeys TRUE, convert dictionary keys uppercase distinguish unmatched tokens. exclusive TRUE, remove features dictionary, otherwise, replace values dictionary keys leaving features unaffected. nomatch optional character naming new key tokens matched dictionary values  NULL (default), record unmatched tokens. append_key TRUE, annotate matched tokens keys. separator character separate tokens keys append_key = TRUE. concatenator concatenation character connect words making multi-word sequences. nested_scope treat matches different dictionary keys nested.  one value nested within another, "b" nested within "b c", tokens_lookup() match longer.  nested_scope = "key", longer-match priority applied within key, "dictionary" applies across keys, matching key longer pattern, matches nested within longer pattern keys.  See Details. apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Apply a dictionary to a tokens object — tokens_lookup
text: Dictionary values may consist sequences, different methods counting key matches based values nested overlap. two different keys dictionary nested matches one another, nested_scope options provide choice matching key's values independently ("key") option, just counting longest match ("dictionary" option).  Values nested within key always counted single match.  See last example comparing New York New York Times two different behaviours. Overlapping values, "b" "b " currently always considered separate matches different keys, one match overlap within key. Note: apply_if applies dictionary lookup documents match logical condition.  exclusive = TRUE (default), however, means empty documents returned meeting condition, since lookup applied hence tokens replaced matching keys.

title: Apply a dictionary to a tokens object — tokens_lookup
code: toks1 <- tokens(data_corpus_inaugural) dict1 <- dictionary(list(country = "united states",                    law=c("law*", "constitution"),                    freedom=c("free*", "libert*"))) dfm(tokens_lookup(toks1, dict1, valuetype = "glob", verbose = TRUE)) #> tokens_lookup() changed from 10,332 types (60 documents, 154,888 tokens) to 3 types (60 documents, 1,211 tokens) #> Document-feature matrix of: 60 documents, 3 features (14.44% sparse) and 4 #> docvars. #>                  features #> docs              country law freedom #>   1789-Washington       2   1       6 #>   1793-Washington       0   1       0 #>   1797-Adams            3  10       6 #>   1801-Jefferson        0   6      11 #>   1805-Jefferson        1  13       6 #>   1809-Madison          2   2       5 #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 54 more documents ] dfm(tokens_lookup(toks1, dict1, valuetype = "glob", verbose = TRUE, nomatch = "NONE")) #> tokens_lookup() changed from 10,332 types (60 documents, 154,888 tokens) to 4 types (60 documents, 154,723 tokens) #> Document-feature matrix of: 60 documents, 4 features (10.83% sparse) and 4 #> docvars. #>                  features #> docs              country law freedom none #>   1789-Washington       2   1       6 1526 #>   1793-Washington       0   1       0  146 #>   1797-Adams            3  10       6 2555 #>   1801-Jefferson        0   6      11 1906 #>   1805-Jefferson        1  13       6 2359 #>   1809-Madison          2   2       5 1250 #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 54 more documents ]  dict2 <- dictionary(list(country = "united states",                        law = c("law", "constitution"),                        freedom = c("freedom", "liberty"))) # dfm(applyDictionary(toks1, dict2, valuetype = "fixed")) dfm(tokens_lookup(toks1, dict2, valuetype = "fixed")) #> Document-feature matrix of: 60 documents, 3 features (20.56% sparse) and 4 #> docvars. #>                  features #> docs              country law freedom #>   1789-Washington       2   1       1 #>   1793-Washington       0   1       0 #>   1797-Adams            3   8       3 #>   1801-Jefferson        0   6       7 #>   1805-Jefferson        1   9       5 #>   1809-Madison          2   2       3 #> [ reached max_ndoc ... 54 more documents ]  # hierarchical dictionary example txt <- c(d1 = "The United States has the Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean.",          d2 = "Britain and Ireland have the Irish Sea and the English Channel.") toks2 <- tokens(txt) dict3 <- dictionary(list(US = list(Countries = c("States"),                                   oceans = c("Atlantic", "Pacific")),                         Europe = list(Countries = c("Britain", "Ireland"),                                       oceans = list(west = "Irish Sea",                                                     east = "English Channel")))) tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = 1) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "US" "US" "US" #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "Europe" "Europe" "Europe" "Europe" #>  tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = 2) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "Countries" "oceans"    "oceans"    #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "Countries" "Countries" "oceans"    "oceans"    #>  tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = 1:2) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "US.Countries" "US.oceans"    "US.oceans"    #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "Europe.Countries" "Europe.Countries" "Europe.oceans"    "Europe.oceans"    #>  tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = 3) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> character(0) #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "west" "east" #>  tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = c(1,3)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "US" "US" "US" #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "Europe"      "Europe"      "Europe.west" "Europe.east" #>  tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, levels = c(2,3)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #> [1] "Countries" "oceans"    "oceans"    #>  #> d2 : #> [1] "Countries"   "Countries"   "oceans.west" "oceans.east" #>   # show unmatched tokens tokens_lookup(toks2, dict3, nomatch = "_UNMATCHED") #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> d1 : #>  [1] "_UNMATCHED"   "_UNMATCHED"   "US.Countries" "_UNMATCHED"   "_UNMATCHED"   #>  [6] "US.oceans"    "_UNMATCHED"   "_UNMATCHED"   "_UNMATCHED"   "US.oceans"    #> [11] "_UNMATCHED"   "_UNMATCHED"   #>  #> d2 : #>  [1] "Europe.Countries"   "_UNMATCHED"         "Europe.Countries"   #>  [4] "_UNMATCHED"         "_UNMATCHED"         "Europe.oceans.west" #>  [7] "_UNMATCHED"         "_UNMATCHED"         "Europe.oceans.east" #> [10] "_UNMATCHED"         #>   # nested matching differences dict4 <- dictionary(list(paper = "New York Times", city = "New York")) toks4 <- tokens("The New York Times is a New York paper.") tokens_lookup(toks4, dict4, nested_scope = "key", exclusive = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"   "PAPER" "CITY"  "is"    "a"     "CITY"  "paper" "."     #>  tokens_lookup(toks4, dict4, nested_scope = "dictionary", exclusive = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"   "PAPER" "is"    "a"     "CITY"  "paper" "."     #>

code: txt <- c("This is text one", "The text two", "This is text three") (toks1 <- tokens(txt[1:2])) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "This" "is"   "text" "one"  #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "The"  "text" "two"  #>  (toks2 <- tokens(txt[2:3])) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "The"  "text" "two"  #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "This"  "is"    "text"  "three" #>  (toks3 <- tokens_match(toks1, types(toks2))) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "This" "is"   "text" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "The"  "text" "two"  #>  identical(types(toks2), types(toks3)) #> [1] TRUE

code: tokens_ngrams(   x,   n = 2L,   skip = 0L,   concatenator = concat(x),   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )  char_ngrams(x, n = 2L, skip = 0L, concatenator = "_")  tokens_skipgrams(   x,   n,   skip,   concatenator = concat(x),   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
text: x tokens object, character vector, list characters n integer vector specifying number elements concatenated n-gram.  element vector define \(n\) \(n\)-gram(s) produced. skip integer vector specifying adjacency skip size tokens forming n-grams, default 0 immediately neighbouring words. skipgrams, skip can vector integers, "classic" approach forming skip-grams set skip = \(k\) \(k\) distance \(k\) fewer skips used construct \(n\)-gram.  Thus "4-skip-n-gram" defined skip = 0:4 produces results include 4 skips, 3 skips, 2 skips, 1 skip, 0 skips (0 skips typical n-grams formed adjacent words).  See Guthrie et al (2006). concatenator character combining words, default _ (underscore) character apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
text: tokens object consisting list character vectors n-grams, one list element per text, character vector called simple character vector

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
text: Normally, functions called [tokens](x, ngrams = , ...), functions provided case user wants perform lower-level n-gram construction tokenized texts. tokens_skipgrams() wrapper tokens_ngrams() requires arguments supplied n skip. \(k\)-skip skip-grams, set skip 0:\(k\), order conform definition skip-grams found Guthrie et al (2006): \(k\) skip-gram n-gram superset n-grams \((k-)\) skip-gram \((k-)==0\) (includes 0 skip-grams).

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
text: char_ngrams convenience wrapper (non-list) vector characters, named consistent quanteda's naming scheme.

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
text: Guthrie, David, Ben Allison, Wei Liu, Louise Guthrie, Yorick Wilks. 2006. "Closer Look Skip-Gram Modelling." https://aclanthology.org/L06-1210/

title: Create n-grams and skip-grams from tokens — tokens_ngrams
code: # ngrams tokens_ngrams(tokens(c("a b c d e", "c d e f g")), n = 2:3) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "a_b"   "b_c"   "c_d"   "d_e"   "a_b_c" "b_c_d" "c_d_e" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "c_d"   "d_e"   "e_f"   "f_g"   "c_d_e" "d_e_f" "e_f_g" #>   toks <- tokens(c(text1 = "the quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog")) tokens_ngrams(toks, n = 1:3) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #>  [1] "the"         "quick"       "brown"       "fox"         "jumped"      #>  [6] "over"        "the"         "lazy"        "dog"         "the_quick"   #> [11] "quick_brown" "brown_fox"   #> [ ... and 12 more ] #>  tokens_ngrams(toks, n = c(2,4), concatenator = " ") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #>  [1] "the quick"              "quick brown"            "brown fox"              #>  [4] "fox jumped"             "jumped over"            "over the"               #>  [7] "the lazy"               "lazy dog"               "the quick brown fox"    #> [10] "quick brown fox jumped" "brown fox jumped over"  "fox jumped over the"    #> [ ... and 2 more ] #>  tokens_ngrams(toks, n = c(2,4), skip = 1, concatenator = " ") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #>  [1] "the brown"            "quick fox"            "brown jumped"         #>  [4] "fox over"             "jumped the"           "over lazy"            #>  [7] "the dog"              "the brown jumped the" "quick fox over lazy"  #> [10] "brown jumped the dog" #>  # skipgrams toks <- tokens("insurgents killed in ongoing fighting") tokens_skipgrams(toks, n = 2, skip = 0:1, concatenator = " ") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "insurgents killed" "insurgents in"     "killed in"         #> [4] "killed ongoing"    "in ongoing"        "in fighting"       #> [7] "ongoing fighting"  #>  tokens_skipgrams(toks, n = 2, skip = 0:2, concatenator = " ") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "insurgents killed"  "insurgents in"      "insurgents ongoing" #> [4] "killed in"          "killed ongoing"     "killed fighting"    #> [7] "in ongoing"         "in fighting"        "ongoing fighting"   #>  tokens_skipgrams(toks, n = 3, skip = 0:2, concatenator = " ") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #>  [1] "insurgents killed in"        "insurgents killed ongoing"   #>  [3] "insurgents killed fighting"  "insurgents in ongoing"       #>  [5] "insurgents in fighting"      "insurgents ongoing fighting" #>  [7] "killed in ongoing"           "killed in fighting"          #>  [9] "killed ongoing fighting"     "in ongoing fighting"         #>

what: Replace tokens in a tokens object — tokens_replace
title: Replace tokens in a tokens object — tokens_replace
text: Substitute token types based vectorized one--one matching. Since function created lemmatization user-defined stemming. supports substitution multi-word features multi-word features, substitution fastest pattern replacement character vectors valuetype = "fixed" function substitute types tokens. Please use tokens_lookup() exclusive = FALSE replace dictionary values.

title: Replace tokens in a tokens object — tokens_replace
code: tokens_replace(   x,   pattern,   replacement,   valuetype = "glob",   case_insensitive = TRUE,   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Replace tokens in a tokens object — tokens_replace
text: x tokens object whose token elements replaced pattern character vector list character vectors.  See pattern details. replacement character vector (pattern list) list character vectors length pattern valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Replace tokens in a tokens object — tokens_replace
code: toks1 <- tokens(data_corpus_inaugural, remove_punct = TRUE)  # lemmatization taxwords <- c("tax", "taxing", "taxed", "taxed", "taxation") lemma <- rep("TAX", length(taxwords)) toks2 <- tokens_replace(toks1, taxwords, lemma, valuetype = "fixed") kwic(toks2, "TAX") |>     tail(10) #> Keyword-in-context with 10 matches. #>                                                                        #>   [1981-Reagan, 273]                      for their labor by a | TAX | #>   [1981-Reagan, 290]             productivity But great as our | TAX | #>  [1981-Reagan, 1521]               and to lighten our punitive | TAX | #>   [1985-Reagan, 496]                were right to believe that | TAX | #>  [1985-Reagan, 1106]                lives We must simplify our | TAX | #>  [1985-Reagan, 1418] permanently control Government's power to | TAX | #>  [1985-Reagan, 1438]              spend its citizens money and | TAX | #>    [2013-Obama, 739]          remake our government revamp our | TAX | #>   [2025-Trump, 1536]           workers and families Instead of | TAX | #>   [2025-Trump, 1547]              countries we will tariff and | TAX | #>                                                #>  system which penalizes successful achievement #>  burden is it has not                          #>  burden And these will be                      #>  rates have been reduced inflation             #>  system make it more fair                      #>  and spend We must act                         #>  them into servitude when the                  #>  Code reform our schools and                   #>  our citizens to enrich other                  #>  foreign countries to enrich our                # stemming type <- types(toks1) stem <- char_wordstem(type, "porter") toks3 <- tokens_replace(toks1, type, stem, valuetype = "fixed", case_insensitive = FALSE) identical(toks3, tokens_wordstem(toks1, "porter")) #> [1] TRUE  # multi-multi substitution toks4 <- tokens_replace(toks1, phrase(c("Supreme Court")),                         phrase(c("Supreme Court of the United States"))) kwic(toks4, phrase(c("Supreme Court of the United States"))) #> Keyword-in-context with 5 matches. #>                                                                #>   [1857-Buchanan, 441:446] which legitimately belongs to the | #>  [1861-Lincoln, 2323:2328]              to be decided by the | #>  [1861-Lincoln, 2465:2470]         fixed by decisions of the | #>   [1889-Harrison, 408:413]        by the organization of the | #>        [2025-Trump, 27:32]   Justice Roberts justices of the | #>                                                                         #>  Supreme Court of the United States | of the United States before       #>  Supreme Court of the United States | nor do I deny that                #>  Supreme Court of the United States | the instant they are made         #>  Supreme Court of the United States | shall have been suitably observed #>  Supreme Court of the United States | of the United States President

what: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
title: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
text: Segment tokens splitting pattern match. useful breaking tokenized texts smaller document units, based regular pattern user-supplied annotation. normally makes sense corpus level (see corpus_segment()), tokens_segment provides option perform operation tokens.

title: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
code: tokens_segment(   x,   pattern,   valuetype = c("glob", "regex", "fixed"),   case_insensitive = TRUE,   extract_pattern = FALSE,   pattern_position = c("before", "after"),   use_docvars = TRUE,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
text: x tokens object whose token elements segmented pattern character vector, list character vectors, dictionary, collocations object.  See pattern details. valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values extract_pattern remove matched patterns texts save docvars, TRUE pattern_position either "" "", depending whether pattern precedes text (tag) follows text (punctuation delimiters) use_docvars TRUE, repeat docvar values segmented text; FALSE, drop docvars segmented corpus. Dropping docvars might useful order conserve space desired segmented corpus. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
text: tokens_segment returns tokens object whose documents split patterns

title: Segment tokens object by patterns — tokens_segment
code: txts <- "Fellow citizens, I am again called upon by the voice of my country to execute the functions of its Chief Magistrate. When the occasion proper for it shall arrive, I shall endeavor to express the high sense I entertain of this distinguished honor." toks <- tokens(txts)  # split by any punctuation tokens_segment(toks, "^\\p{Sterm}$", valuetype = "regex",                extract_pattern = TRUE,                pattern_position = "after") #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> text1.1 : #>  [1] "Fellow"   "citizens" ","        "I"        "am"       "again"    #>  [7] "called"   "upon"     "by"       "the"      "voice"    "of"       #> [ ... and 10 more ] #>  #> text1.2 : #>  [1] "When"     "the"      "occasion" "proper"   "for"      "it"       #>  [7] "shall"    "arrive"   ","        "I"        "shall"    "endeavor" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  tokens_segment(toks, c(".", "?", "!"), valuetype = "fixed",                extract_pattern = TRUE,                pattern_position = "after") #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> text1.1 : #>  [1] "Fellow"   "citizens" ","        "I"        "am"       "again"    #>  [7] "called"   "upon"     "by"       "the"      "voice"    "of"       #> [ ... and 10 more ] #>  #> text1.2 : #>  [1] "When"     "the"      "occasion" "proper"   "for"      "it"       #>  [7] "shall"    "arrive"   ","        "I"        "shall"    "endeavor" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>

what: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
title: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
text: function select discard tokens tokens object.  convenience, functions tokens_remove tokens_keep defined shortcuts tokens_select(x, pattern, selection = "remove") tokens_select(x, pattern, selection = "keep"), respectively.  common usage tokens_remove eliminate stop words text text-based object, common use tokens_select select tokens positive pattern matches list regular expressions, including dictionary. startpos endpos determine positions tokens searched pattern areas affected expanded window.

title: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
code: tokens_select(   x,   pattern,   selection = c("keep", "remove"),   valuetype = c("glob", "regex", "fixed"),   case_insensitive = TRUE,   padding = FALSE,   window = 0,   min_nchar = NULL,   max_nchar = NULL,   startpos = 1L,   endpos = -1L,   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )  tokens_remove(x, ...)  tokens_keep(x, ...)

title: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
text: x tokens object whose token elements removed kept pattern character vector, list character vectors, dictionary, collocations object.  See pattern details. selection whether "keep" "remove" tokens matching pattern valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. case_insensitive logical; TRUE, ignore case matching pattern dictionary values padding TRUE, leave empty string removed tokens previously existed.  useful positional match needed pre- post-selected tokens, instance window adjacency needs computed. window integer length 1 2; size window tokens adjacent pattern selected. window symmetric unless vector two elements supplied, case first element token length window pattern, second token length window pattern. default 0, meaning pattern matched token(s) selected, adjacent terms. Terms overlapping windows never double-counted, simply returned pattern match. tokens_select never redefines document units; , see kwic(). min_nchar, max_nchar optional numerics specifying minimum maximum length characters tokens removed kept; defaults NULL limits.  applied (hence, addition ) selection based pattern matches. startpos, endpos integer; position tokens documents pattern matching starts ends, 1 first token document.  negative indexes, counting starts ending token document, -1 denotes last token document, -2 second last, etc. length vector equal ndoc, tokens corresponding positions selected; less ndoc, values repeated make equal length. apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings. ... additional arguments passed tokens_remove tokens_keep tokens_select. include selection.

title: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
text: tokens object tokens selected removed based match pattern

title: Select or remove tokens from a tokens object — tokens_select
code: ## tokens_select with simple examples toks <- as.tokens(list(letters, LETTERS)) tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "keep", padding = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "b" "e" "f" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "B" "E" "F" #>  tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "keep", padding = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] ""  "b" ""  ""  "e" "f" ""  ""  ""  ""  ""  ""  #> [ ... and 14 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] ""  "B" ""  ""  "E" "F" ""  ""  ""  ""  ""  ""  #> [ ... and 14 more ] #>  tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "remove", padding = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "a" "c" "d" "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "A" "C" "D" "G" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" "M" "N" "O" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "remove", padding = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "a" ""  "c" "d" ""  ""  "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" #> [ ... and 14 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "A" ""  "C" "D" ""  ""  "G" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" #> [ ... and 14 more ] #>   # how case_insensitive works tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "remove", case_insensitive = TRUE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "a" "c" "d" "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "A" "C" "D" "G" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" "M" "N" "O" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  tokens_select(toks, c("b", "e", "f"), selection = "remove", case_insensitive = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "a" "c" "d" "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" #> [ ... and 11 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "A" "B" "C" "D" "E" "F" "G" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" #> [ ... and 14 more ] #>   # use window tokens_select(toks, c("b", "f"), selection = "keep", window = 1) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "a" "b" "c" "e" "f" "g" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "A" "B" "C" "E" "F" "G" #>  tokens_select(toks, c("b", "f"), selection = "remove", window = 1) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "d" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" "p" "q" "r" #> [ ... and 8 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "D" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" "M" "N" "O" "P" "Q" "R" #> [ ... and 8 more ] #>  tokens_remove(toks, c("b", "f"), window = c(0, 1)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "a" "d" "e" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" "p" #> [ ... and 10 more ] #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "A" "D" "E" "H" "I" "J" "K" "L" "M" "N" "O" "P" #> [ ... and 10 more ] #>  tokens_select(toks, pattern = c("e", "g"), window = c(1, 2)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "d" "e" "f" "g" "h" "i" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "D" "E" "F" "G" "H" "I" #>   # tokens_remove example: remove stopwords txt <- c(wash1 <- "Fellow citizens, I am again called upon by the voice of my                    country to execute the functions of its Chief Magistrate.",          wash2 <- "When the occasion proper for it shall arrive, I shall                    endeavor to express the high sense I entertain of this                    distinguished honor.") tokens_remove(tokens(txt, remove_punct = TRUE), stopwords("english")) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #>  [1] "Fellow"     "citizens"   "called"     "upon"       "voice"      #>  [6] "country"    "execute"    "functions"  "Chief"      "Magistrate" #>  #> text2 : #>  [1] "occasion"      "proper"        "shall"         "arrive"        #>  [5] "shall"         "endeavor"      "express"       "high"          #>  [9] "sense"         "entertain"     "distinguished" "honor"         #>   # token_keep example: keep two-letter words tokens_keep(tokens(txt, remove_punct = TRUE), "??") #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents. #> text1 : #> [1] "am" "by" "of" "my" "to" "of" #>  #> text2 : #> [1] "it" "to" "of" #>

what: Split tokens by a separator pattern — tokens_split
title: Split tokens by a separator pattern — tokens_split
text: Replaces tokens multiple replacements consisting elements split separator pattern, option retaining separator.  function effectively reverses operation tokens_compound().

title: Split tokens by a separator pattern — tokens_split
code: tokens_split(   x,   separator = " ",   valuetype = c("fixed", "regex"),   remove_separator = TRUE,   apply_if = NULL,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose") )

title: Split tokens by a separator pattern — tokens_split
text: x tokens object separator single-character pattern match tokens separated valuetype type pattern matching: "glob" "glob"-style wildcard expressions; "regex" regular expressions; "fixed" exact matching. See valuetype details. remove_separator TRUE, remove separator new tokens apply_if logical vector length ndoc(x); documents modified corresponding values TRUE, others left unchanged. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings.

title: Split tokens by a separator pattern — tokens_split
code: # undo tokens_compound() toks1 <- tokens("pork barrel is an idiomatic multi-word expression") tokens_compound(toks1, phrase("pork barrel")) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "pork_barrel" "is"          "an"          "idiomatic"   "multi-word"  #> [6] "expression"  #>  tokens_compound(toks1, phrase("pork barrel")) |>     tokens_split(separator = "_") #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #> [1] "pork"       "barrel"     "is"         "an"         "idiomatic"  #> [6] "multi-word" "expression" #>   # similar to tokens(x, remove_hyphen = TRUE) but post-tokenization toks2 <- tokens("UK-EU negotiation is not going anywhere as of 2018-12-24.") tokens_split(toks2, separator = "-", remove_separator = FALSE) #> Tokens consisting of 1 document. #> text1 : #>  [1] "UK"          "-"           "EU"          "negotiation" "is"          #>  [6] "not"         "going"       "anywhere"    "as"          "of"          #> [11] "2018"        "-"           #> [ ... and 4 more ] #>

what: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
title: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
text: Returns document subsets tokens meet certain conditions, including direct logical operations docvars (document-level variables). tokens_subset() functions identically subset.data.frame(), using non-standard evaluation evaluate conditions based docvars tokens.

title: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
code: tokens_subset(   x,   subset,   min_ntoken = NULL,   max_ntoken = NULL,   drop_docid = TRUE,   verbose = quanteda_options("verbose"),   ... )

title: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
text: x tokens object subsetted. subset logical expression indicating documents keep: missing values taken false. min_ntoken, max_ntoken minimum maximum lengths documents extract. drop_docid TRUE, docid documents removed result subsetting. verbose TRUE print number tokens documents function applied. number tokens include paddings. ... used

title: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
text: tokens object, subset documents (docvars) selected according arguments

title: Extract a subset of a tokens — tokens_subset
code: corp <- corpus(c(d1 = "a b c d", d2 = "a a b e",                  d3 = "b b c e", d4 = "e e f a b"),                  docvars = data.frame(grp = c(1, 1, 2, 3))) toks <- tokens(corp) # selecting on a docvars condition tokens_subset(toks, grp > 1) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> d3 : #> [1] "b" "b" "c" "e" #>  #> d4 : #> [1] "e" "e" "f" "a" "b" #>  # selecting on a supplied vector tokens_subset(toks, c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)) #> Tokens consisting of 2 documents and 1 docvar. #> d1 : #> [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" #>  #> d3 : #> [1] "b" "b" "c" "e" #>
